variants.rewritten_question_text: An airplane in flight is subject to an air resistance force proportional to the square of its speed \( v \). Air flowing over the wings is pushed down and slightly forward, so from Newton's third law the air exerts a force on the wings and airplane that is up and slightly backward as shown in the figure. The upward force is the lift force that keeps the airplane aloft, and the backward force is called induced drag. At flying speeds, induced drag is inversely proportional to \( v^2 \), so the total air resistance force can be expressed by \( F_{air} = \alpha v^2 + \beta / v^2 \), where \( \alpha \) and \( \beta \) are positive constants that depend on the shape and size of the airplane and the density of the air. For a Cessna 150, a small single-engine airplane, \( \alpha = 0.30 \, \mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{s}^2 / \mathrm{m}^2 \) and \( \beta = 3.5 \times 10^5 \, \mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^2 / \mathrm{s}^2 \). In steady flight, the engine must provide a forward force that exactly balances the air resistance force. Calculate the speed, in km/h, for which the airplane will have the maximum endurance (that is, remain in the air the longest time).

This exercise is performed by a \(750\,\text{N}\) person as shown in the figure. When he bends his body at the hip, his legs, trunk, head, and arms have the dimensions indicated. Furthermore, his legs and feet weigh a total of \(277\,\text{N}\), and their center of mass is \(41\,\text{cm}\) from his hip, measured along his legs. The person’s trunk, head, and arms weigh \(473\,\text{N}\), and their center of gravity is \(65\,\text{cm}\) from his hip, measured along the upper body. Find the friction force on each foot and on each hand, assuming that it is the same on both feet and on both hands. Give your answer in newtons.

rationale: The question is already open-ended and does not rely on option labels. Keeping it preserves the original physics task and answer granularity.

variants.rewritten_question_text: A gate weighs \(700\,\text{N}\). Its center of gravity is at its center. To relieve the strain on the top hinge, a wire is connected as shown in the figure. The tension in \(CD\) is increased until the horizontal force at hinge \(A\) is zero. What is the combined vertical force exerted by hinges \(A\) and \(B\)?

variants.rewritten_question_text: One end of a uniform meter stick is placed against a vertical wall. The coefficient of static friction between the end of the meter stick and the wall is \(0.40\). When \(\theta = 15^\circ\), how large must the coefficient of static friction be so that the block can be attached \(10\,\text{cm}\) from the left end of the stick without causing it to slip?

rationale: The question is already open-ended in substance; removing the multiple-choice options preserves the original numeric physics task without changing its semantics.

variants.rewritten_question_text: Two identical, uniform beams weighing \(260\,\text{N}\) each are connected at one end by a frictionless hinge. A light horizontal crossbar attached at the midpoints of the beams maintains an angle of \(53.0^\circ\) between the beams. The beams are suspended from the ceiling by vertical wires. What force does the crossbar exert on each beam? Give your answer in newtons.
